titleStmt.title: The Life and Acts of Takla Hāymānot

publicationStmt.publisher: Die Schriftkultur des christlichen Äthiopiens und Eritreas: Eine multimediale Forschungsumgebung / Beta maṣāḥǝft

availability.licence: This file is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0.

msItem.title: Hagiographic Dossier of Takla Hāymānot
Genealogy
Discourse on the translation of Takla Hāymānot
44 miracles of Takla Hāymānot with an introductory discourse
Introduction
msItem.note: Introductory discourse revealed by the Holy Spirit to Yohannes Kama concerning the history of the translation of the body of Takla Haymanot.

msItem.title: The heathen woman and the cross of Takla Hāymānot
msItem.note: translation beginning: There was a certain heathen woman, to whom our father came by night, and he said unto her, "If thou wilt believe in my God I will give thee a sign..."

msItem.title: The healing of a man with crooked legs
msItem.note: And there was a certain man whose legs were crooked, and he was unable to walk upon them, and he came to the church of our father...

msItem.title: The healing of a youth whose arms and legs were twisted
msItem.note: And there was a certain youth whose hands and feet were twisted, and his mother brought him to the place where our father was buried...

msItem.title: Alexander the General and the casting out of the devil
msItem.note: And there was a certain General whose name was Alexander, and he was a man of great power, but he was possessed of a devil...

msItem.title: The wine which became invisible
msItem.note: And it came to pass that when they were celebrating the festival of our father, the wine which they had prepared for the Eucharist became invisible to the eyes of the soldiers...

msItem.title: The fountain which supplied water for the feast of the saint and then disappeared
msItem.note: And there was a great scarcity of water in the place where the people had assembled, and our father caused a fountain of water to spring up...

msItem.title: Water which was turned into olive oil
msItem.note: And again it came to pass that when the oil for the lamps had failed, the priests took water and poured it into the jars, and it became olive oil...

msItem.title: The dough which bred worms
msItem.note: And a certain woman who was preparing the bread for the festival scoffed at the saint, and the dough which she had kneaded straightway bred worms...

msItem.title: Children who went into a fiery furnace and came forth unharmed
msItem.note: And it came to pass that two children fell into a fiery furnace while their mother was baking bread for the feast of the saint...

msItem.title: The crocodile which restored the ox it had seized and wished to eat
msItem.note: And a crocodile seized an ox belonging to the monastery, and the monks cried out in the name of our father Takla Haymanot...

msItem.title: The panther and the child
msItem.note: And a panther carried off a child from his mother's side, and she cried out, "O my father Takla Haymanot, restore to me my son!"

msItem.title: The vulture and the meat
msItem.note: And a vulture carried off the meat which had been prepared for the feast, and the people adjured the bird by the name of the saint...

msItem.title: The unbeliever who was burned to death
msItem.note: And there was a certain man who was an unbeliever, and he mocked the miracles of our father, saying, "Can a dead man do these things?"

msItem.title: Restoration of the bread and wine which had been stolen by soldiers
msItem.note: And certain soldiers came and carried off the bread and wine of the offering, but they were unable to eat or drink thereof...

msItem.title: The panther and the monk
msItem.note: And a panther met a monk in the desert who was being tempted by the devil of fornication, and the beast stood over him...

msItem.title: Woman in childbirth and the barren woman
msItem.note: And there was a woman who had been in labor for three days, and she drank water in which dust from the grave of the saint had been mixed...

msItem.title: Woman who conceived after drinking rain water
msItem.note: And a certain barren woman prayed to our father, and she caught the rain water which fell from the roof of the church and drank it...

msItem.title: The restoration of the possessions of the man of Warab
msItem.note: And a man of the country of Warab lost all his goods, and he came to the monastery and slept there, and our father appeared to him in a dream...

msItem.title: The rainbow which rested over the church for three hours
msItem.note: And it came to pass on the day of the festival that a rainbow of many colors came down from heaven and rested over the tabernacle...

msItem.title: The passage through the river in flood
msItem.note: And the monks were unable to cross the river because of the great flood, and they cried out to our father, and the waters were divided...

msItem.title: The hen of the widow of Angot
msItem.note: There was a certain woman who was a widow in the city of Angot... she went into the market to buy a hen wherewith to celebrate the commemoration of our father...

msItem.title: Casting out of the devil by the saints
msItem.note: And our father appeared unto two monks who were possessed of devils, and he laid his hand upon them and the spirits departed...

msItem.title: The gift of a daughter to the woman of Dembi
msItem.note: And a woman of the country of Dembi had no child, and she vowed that if she had a daughter she would give her to the service of the church...

msItem.title: The healing of a workman's right hand
msItem.note: And a certain workman who was building the church of the saint injured his right hand so that he could not move it...

msItem.title: Sacramental cake which turned into cow's dung
msItem.note: And a man who was unworthy took the sacramental cake to eat it, and as soon as it touched his mouth it turned into cow's dung...

msItem.title: Incense which was brought by a wagha-bird
msItem.note: There was in the country of Amhara a certain God-fearing woman... she said unto the priest, "Bring me some incense for the commemoration of our father..."

msItem.title: The healing of the cripple
msItem.note: And a certain cripple who had been unable to move for many years was carried to the grave of the saint on the day of the translation...

msItem.title: The healing of the sores on a man
msItem.note: And a man who was covered with sores from his head to his feet bathed in the water of the fountain of the saint and was made whole...

msItem.title: Dates which sparkled
msItem.note: And a certain man brought dates as an offering, and as they were being placed before the altar they began to sparkle like stars...

msItem.title: The vulture and the thread
msItem.note: And a vulture carried off a piece of silk thread which a woman was weaving into a garment for the church, and the bird brought it back...

msItem.title: Horses and the grain
msItem.note: And the horses of the king's soldiers entered into the field of the monastery to eat the grain, but their mouths were closed...

msItem.title: The miracle of the mule who refused to eat Takla Hāymānot’s grain
msItem.note: And a certain soldier wished to feed his mule with the grain that had been set aside for the poor, but the mule turned away its head...

msItem.title: Hail storm (Warning to the Husbandman)
msItem.note: "Knowest thou not that this is the day of the death of our father Takla Haymanot?" Then he said, "I met him this very day on my way to the fields..."

msItem.title: Hail storm (Destruction of the Scofers)
msItem.note: And a great hail storm came down and destroyed the cattle and the crops of those who refused to keep the festival of the saint...

msItem.title: Broken plough
msItem.note: And a man who was ploughing on the day of the feast found that his plough was broken in pieces, and he repented of his sin...

msItem.title: Water which was turned into oil
msItem.note: And it came to pass in the country of Katata, on the day of the commemoration... the people placed an earthenware oil jar side by side with one containing water...

msItem.title: Empty oil-jar
msItem.note: Takla Haymanot causes an oil jar, which was almost empty, to become full of oil for the use of the sanctuary...

msItem.title: Vulture and the cock
msItem.note: And a vulture carried off a cock which belonged to a poor man, and the man cried out to the saint, and the bird restored it unharmed...

msItem.title: The mule which refused to drink
msItem.note: And a certain mule, being adjured by the name of Takla Haymanot, refused to drink the water of the pool because it was holy...

msItem.title: The broken plough and the man who was stung by bees
msItem.note: And a man who worked on the Sabbath of the saint was stung by a swarm of bees until he confessed his transgression...

msItem.title: The healing of the paralytic woman
msItem.note: And a woman whose body was dead from the waist downwards was brought to the shrine and was restored to health...

msItem.title: The woman and the broken rods
msItem.note: And a certain man beat his wife with rods because she wished to go to the church, and the rods broke in pieces in his hands...

msItem.title: The man and the panther
msItem.note: And a panther attackaed a man in the desert, and the man called upon the name of our father, and the beast departed into the thicket...

msItem.title: 2 miracles of Takla Hāymānot
The flood which was stopped
msItem.note: And there was a certain man who was carrying the book of the miracles of our father across a river, and a great flood came down and surrounded him...

msItem.title: Woman who was healed
msItem.note: And a certain woman who was a daughter of the nobility was smitten with a sore disease of the eyes, and she came to the shrine and was healed by the dust of the grave...

supportDesc.foliation: Foliation by the library in the upper right corner.

layout.ab: Primary pricks and horizontal pricks are visible.
The upper line is written above the ruling, the lower line is written above the ruling.

handNote.desc: The manuscript is written in good hand, of the 18th century.

decoNote.desc: Holy Trinity
Takla Haymanot at the head of the company of the Archangels and Angels in Heaven
Saint George spearing the dragon
Our Lady, the Virgin Mary, and the Child, with Saint Michael and Saint Gabriel as attendants
Christ the Lord, with the crown of thorns on His head
(1) The Crucifixion of Christ on Golgotha; on the left stands the Virgin Mary, and on the right Saint John, (2) The body of Christ being made ready for burial (3) The Ascension of Christ into heaven
Saga Za'ab bringing an offering to the church; Saga Za'ab burning incense in the church; Egzi'e Haraya, the wife of Saga Za'ab, praying for a son
Saga Za'ab celebrating a festival in honour of Saint Michael; Egzi'e Haraya (his wife) in attendance
Rescue by Saint Michael of Saga Za'ab, who had fallen into a lake during his flight from his city when it was captured by Matalome
Egzi'e Haraya, the wife of Saga Za'ab, being carried off into captivity by the troops of Matalome
Matalome, having attempted to marry Egzi'e Haraya, is prevented from doing so by Saint Michael, and is smitten with madness
Saga Za'ab recognizing his wife, who makes herself known to him at the door of the temple
The visions of Saga Za'ab and Egzi'e Haraya, and Saint Michael explaining them
Egzi'e Haraya nursing her newly born child, who was called Feshha Seyon, and subsequently Takla Haymanot
The descent of the Holy Spirit upon the child three days after his birth, as he was ascribing holiness and unity to the Persons of the Trinity
The child is baptized, and is named Feshha Seyon; Saint Michael tells Saga Za'ab that his child shall later be called by another name
The child Feshha Seyon, above whom is his guardian angel, supplying his mother's house with flour, oil, and wine during a season of famine
The child Feshha Seyon touching the oil-jar, which becomes full
The guests seated at table marvelling at the sumptuous meal
The child Feshha Seyon being taught the Scriptures under the guidance of the Holy Spirit
Feshha Seyon is ordained deacon by Cyril, Bishop of Alexandria, during the patriarchate of Benjamin, Archbishop of Ethiopia
Saint Michael, seeing a man beating Feshha Seyon, seizes him and suspends him in the air, head downwards
Two sick persons are healed by the water in which Feshha Seyon has washed his feet, and the devil leaves them in the form of an ape
Feshha Seyon causes a spring of water to rise from the ground and multiplies the flour in a bowl
Feshha Seyon refuses to marry the maiden chosen by his parents; the death of the maiden who had been chosen for his bride
Saint Michael commands Feshha Seyon to give up hunting animals and to hunt the souls of men
Feshha Seyon gives all his possessions to the poor
Bishop Cyril ordains Feshha Seyon high-priest of Shawa and bestows upon him the name of Takla Haymanot
At the time of Feshha Seyon’s ordination, Saint Michael appears to his mother and tells her that he has fulfilled all her desires because she kept his festivals with faith and reverence
Christ breathes in Takla Haymanot’s face three times, and Saint Michael gives him the sword of the Spirit
The death of Takla Haymanot’s mother; the death of Takla Haymanot’s father
Saint Michael gives Takla Haymanot a vessel of the oil of meiron with which he is to anoint Yakuno ’Amlak king; Takla Haymanot anoints Yakuno ’Amlak king
Takla Haymanot preaching to the magicians of the country of Katata
A tree-devil who is worshipped by the people of Katata reviles Takla Haymanot
The tree, having torn itself up by its roots from the ground by the command of the saint, came to him, and twenty-four men die of fright
The disappearance of the devil in clouds of smoke; the men of Katata come to Takla Haymanot and bow in submission at his feet
Takla Haymanot baptizing the men of Katata
The governor of the city is blinded by a splinter from the devil's tree; Takla Haymanot removes the splinter and restores sight to the eye
At the command of Takla Haymanot the devil departs to his own place; Takla Haymanot raising to life those who had been long dead
Takla Haymanot bidding the people of Katata farewell; Takla Haymanot travelling on a chariot of light
Takla Haymanot conversing with the men of Wifat; The devils of Wifat mounted on fire-breathing wolves
Takla Haymanot smites the king-magician of Bilat on the face; the king’s guards beating Takla Haymanot to death; Saint Michael raising Takla Haymanot to life
Takla Haymanot curses the magicians; the magicians and their king in the pit of Sheol
The fragments of the idols which Takla Haymanot dashed in pieces
God’s hand stretching itself out while the devils are reviling and stoning Takla Haymanot; Takla Haymanot breaking in pieces the idols of Zeba Fetan
Takla Haymanot is beaten for smashing the idols; Takla Haymanot casts out an evil spirit from the son of Karar Wedem, the governor
Takla Haymanot baptizes the governor, and his wife, and son
Takla Haymanot and the governor are taken, fettered, into the presence of the king
The soldiers accusing the saint and the governor; Takla Haymanot and Karar Wedem in the king’s presence
Takla Haymanot and Karar Wedem are hurled in baskets down a precipice, but are caught in mid-air by Saint Michael
King Matalome seated with the baskets before him; Takla Haymanot and the governor reappear unharmed
King Matalome tries to spear Takla Haymanot, but only wounds himself; Takla Haymanot addressing the people
King Matalome’s spearmen slaying the men who were converted by Takla Haymanot
Matalome’s subjects entreating him to hang the saint; the hanging of Takla Haymanot - the tree deposits him safely on the ground, but the hangman falls and is killed
The slaughter of the men who became Christians when they saw the tree deposit Takla Haymanot in safety on the ground
Takla Haymanot healing the wounds in the king's hands; Takla Haymanot and Karar Wedem conversing with the magicians
King Matalome spears three magicians, and they fall down dead
King Matalome entreating Takla Haymanot to transfer to him his baptismal name of Feshha Seyon
Priests from Shawa confessing that they worshipped idols only because they were beaten and cast into prison
King Matalome listening to the counsel of the magician; Takla Haymanot standing up unscathed at the trial by fire, while all the magicians are consumed
Takla Haymanot baptizing the king and his followers, and changing Matalome’s name to Feshha Seyon
The king receives the offering after a fast of three days
The king gives one thousand pounds’ weight of gold to the saint for his baptismal name of Feshha Seyon; Takla Haymanot gives the gold to the poor and needy
Takla Haymanot discussing the Resurrection with Matalome; Saint Michael raising from the dead those who had been cast into hell when Matalome carried off the saint’s mother
Takla Haymanot giving counsel to Matalome; Takla Haymanot appointing twelve magicians to be preachers of the Gospel
Mary the Virgin, Michael, Gabriel, and the twelve Apostles appearing to Takla Haymanot at Easter
Christ appearing to Takla Haymanot at Easter; Christ giving Takla Haymanot a cup of the water of life
Takla Haymanot bidding the king farewell; Takla Haymanot departing from Damot to Zorare
Takla Haymanot casting out a devil of fornication from a harlot of Zorare
Takla Haymanot and the monk travelling through the air; Takla Haymanot casting out the devil from the son of the master of the house
The monk waking up and finding himself at his own monastery; Takla Haymanot conversing with the abbot
Takla Haymanot’s labours: bringing in a huge load of wood, bringing enough water to fill four buckets at a time, and grinding six measures of flour each day
Takla Haymanot driving out an evil spirit from a man in the presence of the abbot
Takla Haymanot seizes a devil by the throat; Takla Haymanot curing the man who had been possessed - the devil, in the form of an ape, turned into a flash of fire and disappeared
Takla Haymanot raising Basalota Mika'el, the abbot's nephew, from the dead
Takla Haymanot announcing his departure to the abbot
The abbot and the monks bidding Takla Haymanot farewell
Takla Haymanot conversing with a man; Saint Michael and Takla Haymanot walking on the sea
Saint Michael announcing to the abbot Iyasu Mo'a the arrival of Takla Haymanot
Iyasus Mo'a and Takla Haymanot conversing; Saint Michael giving Takla Haymanot power to walk on the sea
Takla Haymanot and Abba Yohanni conversing on the top of a precipitous mountain, which the saint is about to descend by means of a rope forty cubits long
The breaking of the rope as the saint descends: three pairs of wings attach themselves to his shoulders, and by means of these he flies a journey of three days
The departure of Takla Haymanot to the desert of Wali
Takla Haymanot visiting the monks of Dabra Hawzen
Saint Michael makes Takla Haymanot walk on the Red Sea; Takla Haymanot raises to life a pilgrim who died of thirst
Takla Haymanot visiting the Jordan and kneeling before it; Takla Haymanot visiting the monks of Scete; the Angel of the Lord bids Takla Haymanot return to Ethiopia
Takla Haymanot ordaining the man whom he raised from the dead
Takla Haymanot and the Archbishop of Jerusalem conversing
Takla Haymanot and his disciple Ar'ayana Sagahu being received by Abba Yohanni and the monks of Dabra Dammo in Tigray
The Angel of the Lord bids Takla Haymanot to go to the abbot Iyasus Mo'a
Takla Haymanot, having walked over the water to the Monastery of Iyasua Mo'a, which was built on an island, holds converse with the Abbot
Takla Haymanot, by means of the sign of the Cross, rends asunder a horned serpent which was one hundred and seventy-five cubits long
Takla Haymanot, committing the faithful to the care of his disciple, is about to depart for Shawa
A devil warning a certain magician of the coming of Takla Haymanot
Takla Haymanot visiting a magician's cave and driving out a devil; in one portion of the cave he establishes a sacramental tabernacle
Takla Haymanot ordaining his nephew Zena Marqos
Takla Haymanot driving out from his disciple the sea-serpent which entered into him while bathing
Takla Haymanot baptizes a magician and his wife and son
Takla Haymanot assisting Abba Yohanni in counsel; Takla Haymanot holding the Book of the Ordination of Priests and a vessel of the oil of meron
Takla Haymanot grasping the hand of the young man who came to visit him armed
Takla Haymanot causing the magicians of the mountain to be swallowed up by an earthquake in the night
Takla Haymanot scattering the devils which came to his cell to terrify him by night
Takla Haymanot rends asunder the body of a snake seventy cubits long
Takla Haymanot ordains a disciple and calls him Philip; the dead magician of Mount Bera; The church dedicated to Saint Melchisedek
Takla Haymanot appearing to Alexander the general who is smitten with fever
Monks and nuns sleeping together in the Monastery of Takla Haymanot
Takla Haymanot adjuring the wild animals not to devour the crops
Christ, and the Virgin, and the Apostles, and the Prophets appearing to Takla Haymanot in his cell in the desert
Christ explaining to Takla Haymanot the vision of the cloud of light, and of the red and white birds
Takla Haymanot appointing Elisha as his successor
Christ receiving the soul of Takla Haymanot on the death of the saint
Elisha is appointed to be Takla Haymanot's successor; the birds and beasts devouring the crops of the Monastery
The deacon who was dead declaring that he had seen Takla Haymanot in heaven in great glory; Takla Haymanot in his mansion in heaven addressing the deacon Amda Masqal
Abba Philip succeeds Abba Elisha in the Monastery of Takla Haymanot
The Holy Spirit descending upon the grave of Takla Haymanot
The withering of the vines of the Archbishop of Jerusalem
The Archbishop dismissing two of Takla Haymanot's monks
The Abbot Hezekiah at the grave of Takla Haymanot
Takla Haymanot appearing to the Abbot Hezekiah
the translation of the body of Takla Haymanot
A monk summoning the congregation by beating a drum
The congregation assembled for the translation of the body of Takla Haymanot
The Holy Spirit and the Abbot instructing Yohannes Kama concerning the history of Takla Haymanot
Takla Haymanot standing on one leg because the other has dropped off through his standing overmuch
Celebration of the festival of Takla Haymanot
The miracle of the cross of Takla Haymanot
The healing of the cripple
The making straight of the crooked legs of a man
The casting out of the devil
The miracle of the landslip
The miracle of the invisible wine
The miracle of the fountain
The turning of water into olive oil
The dough which bred worms
The child and the heated oven
The crocodile and the ox
A panther seizing a child; Adjured by the name of Takla Haymanot, the panther releases the child and takes him home
The vulture and the meat
An unbeliever who scoffs is burned to death
The restoration of the meat and drink of Marqorewos
A panther attacking a monk who had unclean desires; The monk repents and is absolved by Takla Haymanot
A woman drinking water with dust from the saint’s grave and bringing forth her child; A barren woman drinks water with dust from the saint’s grave and conceives; The woman brings forth her child
A woman drinking rain-water; The woman brings forth her child
Takla Haymanot tells a sleeping man where his property is hidden; The man of Warab finds his goods
Takla Haymanot appearing in the church
Monks passing a river in flood
The vulture carrying off the cock
The miracle of the birds which had been cooked
The scribe writing down the Miracles of Takla Haymanot
Takla Haymanot driving out devils
Takla Haymanot assisting a woman in childbirth
Takla Haymanot drawing out splinters from a man’s hand
The sacramental bread which turned into dung
The bird carrying the incense; The woman telling the priest how the incense arrived
Takla Haymanot healing the bed-ridden man
A man with a skin disease bathes in a stream and is healed
A horse and a mule tell a sick man to go to the shrine of the saint to be healed
The bird restoring the stolen thread
The animals refusing to eat the grain of Takla Haymanot; The same animals eating ordinary grain
Takla Haymanot healing a man by means of his cross
The hailstorm destroying the grain
The hailstorm destroying both cattle and grain
Takla Haymanot warning the husbandman
The turning of water into olive oil
Takla Haymanot creating oil
The vulture and the cock
The mule, being adjured by the name of Takla Haymanot, refuses to drink
The rods used in beating a woman breaking in pieces at the sound of Takla Haymanot’s name
The panther attacking a man and departing to the desert
The breaking of the rods
The healing of a deformed woman
The man with the broken shears stung by bees

item.desc: The name of the owner of the manuscript is .
The note states that the manuscript belongs to the church of .
item.q: ገድለ፡ ተክለ፡ ሃይማኖት፡ ዘቅዱስ፡ መድኃኔ፡ ዓለም።

binding.decoNote: In the original boards, covered with blind-tooled leather and lined with flowered silk.

history.provenance:
The manuscript had very probably belonged to a church in the area, from where it was taken by and brought to . It was then looted by the British Napier expedition in 1868. The manuscript was then sent to the Royal Library at Windsor and presented by to the on the 21st January 1869.
iv . This is the manuscript used for
.

projectDesc.p: Encoded according to TEI P5 Guidelines.

revisionDesc.change: added miracles list and decoDesc data from Budge
